description: Equation (1) provides a method for calculating the grain boundary (GB) energy, \(\gamma_{\mathrm{GB}}\), by isolating the energy contribution attributable to the GBs within a simulated polycrystalline sample. The total potential energy of the system, \(E_{\mathrm{tot}}\), is first reduced by the cohesive energy of all atoms in the face-centered cubic (FCC) crystal structure, given by the product of the number of such atoms, \(N_{\mathrm{FCC}}\), and the cohesive energy per atom, \(E_{0}\). To account for the presence of coherent twin boundaries (CTBs), which possess a known energy, the product of the CTB area, \(A_{\mathrm{CTB}}\), and its specific energy, \(\gamma_{\mathrm{CTB}}\), is also subtracted. The remaining energy is thus ascribed solely to the grain boundaries. Dividing this residual energy by the total grain boundary area, \(A_{\mathrm{GB}}\), yields the average grain boundary energy per unit area.

Equation (2) defines the diffusion coefficient \( D \) for a three-dimensional isotropic system. Here, \( \mathbf{r}(t) \) and \( \mathbf{r}(0) \) denote the position vectors of a given atom at time \( t \) and at the initial reference time, respectively. The quantity \( \left| \mathbf{r}(t) - \mathbf{r}(0) \right|^{2} \) is the squared displacement of the atom over the time interval \( t \). The angle brackets \( \langle \cdots \rangle \) represent an ensemble average, taken over all atoms and over multiple time origins within the simulation to obtain a statistically robust mean. The equation expresses the linear relationship, in the long-time diffusive regime, between the mean squared displacement (MSD) and elapsed time, with the proportionality constant \( D \) scaled by a factor of \( 1/6 \) appropriate for three-dimensional diffusion.

Equation (3) expresses an approximate scaling relationship between the effective diffusivity \(D\) of a polycrystalline material and the grain boundary (GB) diffusivity \(D_{\mathrm{GB}}\). Here, \(D\) represents the macroscopic effective diffusivity, which arises from a combination of lattice and grain boundary diffusion pathways. The quantity \(D_{\mathrm{GB}}\) denotes the intrinsic diffusivity along the grain boundaries, \(\delta\) is the characteristic width of a grain boundary, and \(d\) is the mean grain size. The equation indicates that the effective diffusivity is proportional to the GB diffusivity, scaled by the factor \(2\delta/(d - \delta)\). This factor embodies the geometric influence of the microstructure, where a smaller grain size \(d\) relative to the fixed GB width \(\delta\) enhances the contribution of fast grain boundary pathways to the overall mass transport. The relationship derives from a Maxwell-Garnett-type effective medium approximation, applicable when grain boundaries are treated as high-diffusivity layers embedded within a lower-diffusivity grain interior.

Equation (4) describes the steady-state creep strain rate, \(\dot{\varepsilon}_{\mathrm{c}}\), for a polycrystalline material deforming via Coble creep, a diffusion-controlled mechanism dominant at elevated temperatures and low stresses. In this expression, \(A_{\mathrm{C}}\) is a dimensionless geometric constant, \(\delta\) denotes the effective grain boundary width, \(d\) is the average grain diameter, \(\sigma\) represents the applied tensile stress, \(\Omega_{\mathrm{c}}\) is the atomic volume, \(k_{\mathrm{B}}\) is Boltzmann's constant, \(T\) is the absolute temperature, and \(D_{\mathrm{GB}}\) is the grain boundary diffusion coefficient. The equation captures a linear dependence of the strain rate on the applied stress and the grain boundary diffusivity, while exhibiting a strong inverse cubic dependence on the grain size. This relationship arises because the creep process is governed by the flux of atoms along grain boundary pathways, the density of which scales inversely with grain size.

The equation \(A _ { \mathsf { C } } = 148\) defines a specific numerical value for the geometric pre-factor \(A _ { \mathsf { C } }\), which is a dimensionless constant used in models describing grain boundary diffusion or creep. In the context of the manuscript, this pre-factor appears in a constitutive relation that also involves the grain boundary width \(\delta\), the atomic volume \(\Omega _ { \mathrm { c } }\), and the applied stress \(\sigma\). The assignment of this particular numerical value, as referenced from the literature, serves to calibrate the theoretical model for quantitative analysis, enabling the estimation of other material parameters or deformation rates under given conditions.

Equation (5) provides an approximate implicit representation of the Schwarz D-surface, which acts as the coherent twin boundary (CTB) network separating the 15 constituent parts of the equilibrium unit cell in the modeled polycrystalline metal. Here, \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) denote Cartesian coordinates in three-dimensional space, and \(d\) represents the characteristic aperture of the surface, which corresponds to the grain size of the Schwarz crystal structure. The equation defines the set of points \((x, y, z)\) where the sum of four trigonometric terms—each a product of sine and cosine functions with arguments scaled by \(\pi/(\sqrt{2}d)\)—vanishes. This specific harmonic combination captures the periodic, minimal-surface topology of the Schwarz D-surface, thereby mathematically describing the geometry of the internal interface that partitions the crystalline domain.

equation: $\begin{array} { r } { ( A _ { z } = \frac { 2 c _ { 4 4 } } { c _ { 1 1 } - c _ { 2 2 } } ) } \end{array}$
description: The equation defines the Zener ratio, \(A_z\), a dimensionless parameter used to characterize the degree of elastic anisotropy in cubic crystals. Here, \(c_{11}\), \(c_{22}\), and \(c_{44}\) are the independent elastic stiffness constants of the material. The relationship expresses \(A_z\) as twice the shear modulus \(c_{44}\), divided by the difference between the two principal longitudinal constants, \(c_{11} - c_{22}\). For an elastically isotropic cubic crystal, the Zener ratio equals unity; deviations from this value quantify the magnitude of elastic anisotropy.

The equation represents the Hall–Petch relationship, a classical model describing the grain-size dependence of yield strength in polycrystalline materials. In this expression, \(\sigma_{\mathrm{y}}\) denotes the macroscopic yield strength, \(\sigma_{0}\) is the lattice friction stress required to initiate dislocation motion in a single crystal, \(k\) is the strengthening coefficient (a material-specific constant), and \(d\) represents the average grain diameter. The formulation captures a strengthening regime where the yield stress increases inversely with the square root of the grain size, which is attributed to the role of grain boundaries as barriers to dislocation slip, thereby requiring a higher applied stress to propagate deformation across the polycrystal.

The nonaffine squared displacement \( D_i^2 \) quantifies the local deviation from an ideal affine deformation for a given atom \( i \). In Equation (6), the summation runs over all \( n_i \) neighboring atoms \( j \) within a defined cutoff. The vector \( \mathbf{d}_{ij}^{0} \) denotes the interatomic distance vector between atoms \( i \) and \( j \) in the reference, undeformed configuration, while \( \mathbf{d}_{ij} \) is the corresponding vector in the current, deformed state. The quantity \( \mathbf{J}_i \) represents the best-fit local affine deformation tensor for the neighborhood of atom \( i \), determined by minimizing \( D_i^2 \) itself. The equation thus computes the sum of squared magnitudes of the residual displacement vectors—the differences between the actual observed displacements and those predicted by the optimal affine transformation. Consequently, a larger \( D_i^2 \) indicates a greater degree of nonaffine, disordered atomic rearrangement around atom \( i \), which is associated with processes such as grain boundary migration and sliding in crystalline materials.
